this is the list of new choices on offer make a

grammar will be largely error free and have a good

the sentences were pretty simple at the beginning

this is normal because the talk moved from Simply

and we have a number of interesting elements

voice I was put in charge the beverages were 
included also have what's called a Clift sentence

cleft sentence emphasizes a particular part of a

of threatening to post to review also have you 
noticed a highly complex structure at the very end

if I had been more careful choosing the venue the 
celebration would have turned out differently this

type of sentence is called a third conditional 
sentence I'm discussing a hypothetical situation

in the past if I had been more careful but I 
wasn't and their imagined results the celebration

would have turned out differently so all this is 
unreal The Examiner will definitely notice if you

pull it off or if you get it wrong all in all the 
grammar was good enough for a band eight for a

perfect nine the vocabulary and grammar could have 
been a bit more complex but I wanted to show you

an answer that would be easy to understand and the 
last Criterion is pronunciation we all speak with

an accent that's fine we're not trying to sound 
exactly like nature speakers what's important is

that we can be easily understood our pronunciation 
of individual words is clear and intelligible a

word of advice don't try to use words that you're 
unsure of when it comes to how to pronounce them

if you look through the twom minute talk the word 
aren't any especially difficult words another

aspect is rhythm stress and ination a feature 
of English is how we express meaning through

emphasizing certain words or parts of words take 
this sentence for example which words would you

emphasize most native speakers would probably 
say it like this and when the food finally

they arrived it was either cold or undercooked 
emphasize the most important words and don't

emphasize supporting words such as prepositions 
articles and auxiliary verbs like do it is will

this answer would get a b nine for pronunciation 
although I do have an accent so overall this

answer would get between a b 8 and 8.5 but part 
two is not assessed alone it's part three of

the test that will determine the final score and 
that's where the examiner will push you to your

limit and if you want to get it right learn these 
techniques to give extended high scoring answers

that will impress your examiner and don't forget 
to check out our I speaking Library linked below

thank you so much for watching me today good

transcripts.title: Understand IELTS Writing: Tips and practice questions for Task 1 and 2  | TakeIELTS Preparation

segments.text: ﻿Ready to learn some tips and tricks to help you

improve your IELTS Writing 
test score? OK, let’s go!

Hello and welcome!
My name is Tom and in this

British Council IELTS preparation video, I’m going 
to give you an overview of the IELTS writing test,

share some example questions and give you my top 
tips to help you get the band score you need.
Let’s begin with a quick summary.

There are two types: IELTS General 
Training and IELTS Academic.

IELTS General Training is designed 
for anyone wanting to migrate to

an English-speaking country or to 
study there at below degree level,

whereas IELTS Academic is for those who want to 
study at undergraduate or postgraduate level.

Both Writing tests take 60 minutes, 
and you must complete two tasks.

Let’s start with the IELTS 
General Training Writing test.

In Task 1, you have to respond to 
a situation by writing a letter,
for example, making a request or complaint.

You need to write at least 
150 words in about 20 minutes.

In Task 2, you are given a point of view, argument 
or problem which you need to discuss in an essay.

You need to write at least 
250 words in about 40 minutes.
The topics are based on general interest,

so they are a little less complex 
than in the Academic test.

The IELTS Academic Writing 
test is slightly different.

In Task 1, you have to describe 
some visual information (a graph,
table, chart or diagram) in your own words.

You need to write at least 
150 words in about 20 minutes.

Task 2 is more or less the same 
as the General Training test,

where you are given a point of view, argument or 
problem which you need to discuss in an essay.

Again you’ll need to write at least 
250 words in about 40 minutes.

It’s important to remember that Task 
2 contributes twice as much as Task 1

to the Writing score, so you’ll need to give 
yourself enough time to complete it properly.
What are the Examiners looking for?
Well, they are identifying four things.

In Task 1 the Examiner is 
looking at Task Achievement,

which is essentially - how well 
your letter achieves its purpose.

In Task 2 the Examiner is 
looking at Task Response,

which looks at how well you deal with and 
cover all the main points of the essay.

Are you able to give evidence and examples 
from your own life to support your argument?
Coherence and Cohesion – This is where you display

your ability to write clearly 
and in an organised fashion.

Lexical Resource – This is where 
you show you can use appropriate

vocabulary to communicate your 
ideas accurately and fluently.

Grammatical Range and Accuracy – 
This is about demonstrating your

ability to use a wide variety of grammatical 
structures with as few mistakes as possible.

OK, let’s look in a bit more detail 
at General Training Writing Task 1 –

I’ve got a few tips and tricks 
I’d like to share with you.

The letter you’ll be asked to write 
is always about an everyday situation,

so it could be a complaint, 
a request, a suggestion,

a recommendation, an enquiry, 
an invitation or a thank you.
Here’s an example from IELTS.org.

So, the task is always structured 
with one main paragraph

and three bullet points. The paragraph 
gives us the background situation and the

bullet points are the action points 
you need to mention in your letter.

In the task shown here you need to 
describe the situation, explain the

problem and why it is difficult to work, AND 
say what accommodation you’d prefer instead.

To be able to get a high band score, it isn't 
enough to just touch upon each bullet point.

Test takers need to fully extend their 
answer by adding relevant information.

In order to show that you’ve extended your ideas, 
you need to think of possible problems you might

have with a roommate in college and how that 
might be affecting your ability to study.

So things like – they play music late 
at night, which is affecting your sleep,

or they are constantly inviting friends around and 
disturbing you while you try to study. You should

take 3-5 minutes before you start writing to lay 
out and plan your ideas and what you want to say.

A big part of Task 1 is showing the examiner you 
can use the appropriate tone for your letter.
What does that mean?

Well, there are three types of letters: 
formal, semi-formal and personal.

A formal letter is written to someone 
in authority, for example at a company

or institution, with whom you don’t 
have a relationship. Imagine writing to

a job title, like a hotel manager or bank manager.
A semi-formal letter is written to someone you

know but perhaps you aren’t very close to. It 
could be a boss or teacher, and the subject

matter tends to be somewhat serious in nature.
A personal letter is written to someone you

know well, most likely a friend.
Now, it’s your job to decide

which style of letter you need to write.
There are four questions you can ask yourself:

* Who am I writing to?
* Do I know them?

* What's my relationship with them?
* Why am I writing to them?

So let’s look back at our example:
* Who am I writing to? The accommodation

officer at the college
* Do I know them?

Probably not personally
* What's my relationship

with them? I don’t really have one.
* Why am I writing to them? To make a complaint

and request alternative accommodation.
OK, so all this tells us that we are

going to write a formal letter.
Now, in terms of language content,

what’s the difference between a formal, 
semi-formal and personal letter?

Things you want to consider when writing are:
* How do you open and close the letter?

* What types of words are you going to use?
* Are there any grammatical structures

you should be employing?
Here are a few tips to help you!
When opening a formal letter you should use:

‘Dear Sir or Madam’
because you don’t know who you are writing to.

To close you would say ‘Yours faithfully’.
For a semi-formal letter you could use:

‘Dear (Ms Singh)’ or ‘Dear Sarika’ 
if you are more familiar with them.

And to close use ‘Yours sincerely’.
For personal letters simply put:

‘Dear (first name)’ and close with ‘Best wishes’.
Formal letters tend to be less direct in their

tone, and one way to achieve that is by using 
modal verbs. Compare these two sentences:

‘Would it be possible to find alternative 
accommodation with immediate effect?’

‘Can you get me a new room right now?’
Both sentences are requesting the same thing,

but the first one is using ‘tentative’ language 
to sound less direct and therefore it feels more

polite. They’ve used the modal ‘would’ to create 
that distance between them and the reader. Formal

letters also tend to use passive structures, 
avoid contractions and incorporate more

formal vocabulary – for example, ‘with 
immediate effect’ rather than ‘right now’.

Personal letters can be more direct in their 
tone because you have a close relationship to

the reader. In contrast to formal letters, they 
can use contractions, personal pronouns and tend

to be written in an active voice. In terms 
of vocabulary, it’s acceptable to use more

informal language, for example phrasal verbs 
like ‘take off’ rather than ‘remove’. However,

it’s best to avoid non-standard language, such 
as slang words or the kind of abbreviations you

might use when sending a text message.
Semi-formal letters sit somewhere in

between and incorporate aspects of 
both formal and informal letters.

How are you doing so far?
It’s a lot to take in,

and I really recommend scrolling back 
to review this section again later on.

Alright, here are my quick-fire tips for Task 1.
1. Don’t take more than 20 minutes to do this

task – remember it’s only 33% 
of your total Writing score.

2. You don’t need to write an address 
for this letter, but you must open and

close the letter appropriately.
3. Writing more than 180 words

for this task is a waste of your time, so 
keep the word limit between 150-180 words.

4. Make sure you clearly mention ALL 
three bullet points in your letter.

5. Focus on the aim and purpose of your 
letter – who are you writing to and why?

is always about an everyday situation, so it could be a complaint,

So, the task is always structured with one main paragraph and three bullet 
points. The paragraph gives us the background situation and the bullet points are the action 
points you need to mention in your letter. In the task shown here you need to describe 
the situation, explain the problem and why it’s difficult to work and say what 
accommodation you’d prefer instead. Test takers need to fully extend their 
answer by adding relevant information. In order to show that you’ve extended your ideas, 
you need to think of possible problems you might have with a roommate in college and how that 
might be affecting your ability to study. So things like – they play music late 
at night, which is affecting your sleep, or they are constantly inviting friends around and 
disturbing you while you try to study. You should take 3-5 minutes before you start writing to lay 
out and plan your ideas and what you want to say. A big part of Task 1 is showing the examiner you 
can use the appropriate tone for your letter. What does that mean? Well, there are three types of letters: 
formal, semi-formal and personal. A formal letter is written to someone 
in authority, for example at a company or institution, with whom you don’t have a 
relationship. Imagine writing to a job title, like a hotel manager or bank manager.
A semi-formal letter is written to someone you know but perhaps you aren’t very close to. 
It could be a boss or teacher, and the subject matter tends to be somewhat serious in nature.
A personal letter is written to someone you are close to, most likely a friend.
Now, it’s your job to decide which style of letter you need to write.
Four questions you can ask yourself are: Who am I writing to?
Do I know them? What's my relationship with them?
Why am I writing to them? So let’s look back at our example: Who am I writing to? The 
accommodation officer at the college Do I know them? Probably not personally What's my relationship with 
them? I don’t really have one. Why am I writing to them? To make a complaint 
and request alternative accommodation. OK, so all this tells us that we 
are going to write a formal letter. Now, in terms of language content, 
what’s the difference between a formal, semi-formal and personal letter? Things 
you want to consider when writing are: How do you open and close the letter?
What types of words are you going to use? Are there any grammatical 
structures you should be employing? Here are a few tips to help you!
When opening a formal letter you should use
‘Dear Sir or Madam’ because you don’t know who you are writing to.
To close you would say ‘Yours faithfully’. For a semi-formal letter you could use
‘Dear (Ms Singh)’ or ‘Dear Sarika’ if you are more familiar with them.
And to close use ‘Yours sincerely’. For personal letters simply put
‘Dear (first name)’ and close with ‘Best wishes’. Formal letters tend to be 
less direct in their tone, and one way to achieve that is by using 
modal verbs. Compare these two sentences: ‘Would it be possible to find alternative 
accommodation with immediate effect?’ ‘Can you get me a new room right now?’
Both sentences are requesting the same thing, but the first one is using ‘tentative’ language 
to sound less direct and therefore it feels more polite. They’ve used the modal ‘would’ to 
create that distance between them and the reader. Formal letters also tend to use passive 
structures, avoid contractions and incorporate more formal vocabulary – for example, ‘with 
immediate effect’ rather than ‘right now’. Personal letters can be more direct in their 
tone because you have a close relationship to the reader. In contrast to formal letters, they 
can use contractions, personal pronouns and tend to be written in an active voice. In terms 
of vocabulary, it’s acceptable to use more informal language, for example phrasal verbs 
like ‘take off’ rather than ‘remove’. However, it’s best to avoid non-standard language, such 
as slang words or the kind of abbreviations you might use when sending a text message.
Semi-formal letters sit somewhere in between and incorporate aspects of 
both formal and informal letters. How are you doing so far?
